critic_raw:
**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting (data leakage).** Section 3 states: "all preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit on the entire dataset" explicitly to "avoid instability in vocabulary and scaling parameter estimates due to limited training data." The vocabulary is built from all 10,000 feedback responses across 24 months. This means the training-time text tokenizer has access to vocabulary patterns and clinical terminology that only appear in months 19–24 (the test period), leaking future-period semantic signal into training representations.

**Issue 2: Prior adherence rate is a near-perfect proxy for the outcome label.** Section 3 includes "prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average)" as a metadata feature for both models. The outcome is defined as ≥80% medication adherence over 90 days following the feedback submission (Section 1). A patient with high prior adherence rate is statistically very likely to maintain adherence; a patient with low prior rate is unlikely to recover to ≥80%. If the rolling 6-month adherence rate is computed from the period immediately preceding the feedback submission, it encodes the outcome trajectory. The design states labels are "assigned using clinical data independent of the feedback text" — but prior adherence rate as a feature makes the prediction trivially solved by the metadata baseline, not the sentiment RNN. This undermines the hypothesis that NLP sentiment adds value.

**Issue 3: The test set excludes patients who appeared in training or validation.** Section 2 specifies: "Test set: Months 19–24 (held-out cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation)." If patients must be new to the clinic to appear in the test set, the test population is systematically different from training — newer patients with no prior clinic history, which means the prior adherence rate feature (rolling 6-month average) is unavailable or short for test patients. The model trained on long-tenure patients with rich adherence history is evaluated on new patients with sparse history, creating a distribution shift that the design does not acknowledge.
all_issues_raised: The text tokenizer and vocabulary are fit on all 24 months of feedback before splitting, leaking test-period (months 19–24) clinical vocabulary and terminology patterns into training-time text representations.

Prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average) is included as a metadata feature and closely proxies the ≥80% adherence outcome label — if this feature dominates prediction, the experiment cannot isolate the contribution of sentiment RNN over a simple metadata-based predictor.

The test set is restricted to patients with no prior feedback in training or validation, creating a systematic population shift: test patients are newer to the clinic with sparse adherence history, while training data includes long-tenure patients — this distribution shift is unacknowledged and undermines test-set representativeness.
all_issues_adjudicated: The text tokenizer and vocabulary are fit on all 24 months of feedback before splitting, leaking test-period (months 19–24) clinical vocabulary and terminology patterns into training-time text representations.

Prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average) is included as a metadata feature and closely proxies the ≥80% adherence outcome label — if this feature dominates prediction, the experiment cannot isolate the contribution of sentiment RNN over a simple metadata-based predictor.

The test set is restricted to patients with no prior feedback in training or validation, creating a systematic population shift: test patients are newer to the clinic with sparse adherence history, while training data includes long-tenure patients — this distribution shift is unacknowledged and undermines test-set representativeness.
